title: business onboarding schema
purpose: structured input form for LLM to collect and infer business profile, then match to the correct loyalty program type
instructions: fields marked 'collected' are provided directly by the user. fields marked 'inferred' are derived by the LLM from other inputs — do not ask the user for these. fields marked 'derived' are computed by the LLM after all inputs are collected — never ask the user for these.

sections.description: identifies who the business is and what they sell

fields.label: website url
fields.prompt: do you have a website? if so, paste the url here.
fields.note: if provided, scrape the website to auto-fill services_and_products and industry before asking the user anything

fields.label: services & products
fields.fallback: collected — ask: 'briefly describe what you sell or offer'
fields.example: specialty coffee drinks, beans, brewing equipment
fields.note: extract from homepage, about page, or product/menu pages. summarize into 1-2 sentences. flag whether the core offering is a product, a service, or both — this affects punch card viability.

fields.label: product vs. service
fields.note: infer from services_and_products. used in decision rules: punch card is only viable for single-product or single-service businesses. multi-service businesses cannot cleanly implement punch card logic.

fields.label: industry
fields.fallback: collected — ask: 'what industry are you in?'
fields.note: infer from website or product description. if ambiguous, pick the closest match. used in decision rules as a confirming signal and coalition disqualifier.

fields.label: brand personality
fields.note: inferred from website copy, visual tone, product/service descriptions, and any 'about' or brand language. used downstream by the LLM when customizing program_name, currency_name, brand_voice_summary, and referral_description. do not ask the user for this — if the website is unavailable and services_and_products is sparse, leave as null and fall back to industry-level defaults in the archetype.

tone.description: the dominant emotional register of the brand's communication style
tone.note: pick the single closest match. signals drawn from: headline language, color palette descriptions, product naming style, copy formality. a brand using lowercase everywhere with short punchy copy → irreverent or minimalist. a brand with serif fonts, 'crafted' and 'curated' language → premium. a brand leading with 'our community' or 'our family' → community-driven.

identity_keywords.description: 2–4 short nouns or adjectives that capture what the brand stands for — extracted or closely paraphrased from site copy, not invented
identity_keywords.note: pull from mission statements, taglines, about pages, or product descriptions. if none are available, derive from industry + offering type (e.g. a high-end florist → ['artisan', 'seasonal', 'considered']). keep to 2–4 terms — more than that dilutes signal.

price_positioning.description: where the brand sits on the value spectrum
price_positioning.note: infer from price points if visible on site, or from brand signals: photography quality, language formality, product descriptions. relevant because price positioning affects which rewards feel meaningful — a luxury brand offering '$5 off' feels incongruent; a budget brand offering 'exclusive access' may feel hollow.

customer_relationship_model.description: how the brand frames its relationship with customers
customer_relationship_model.note: transactional = efficient, low-friction, value-focused (think grocery or fuel). community = belonging, shared identity (think local coffee shop or fitness studio). membership = access and exclusivity (think specialty retail or travel). expert-to-customer = trust and guidance (think skincare, financial services, professional services). this maps directly to the tone of brand_voice_summary and the framing of tier names and referral copy.

downstream_usage.program_name: use tone + identity_keywords to generate a name that sounds native to the brand — a brand with tone=irreverent and keywords=['bold','local'] might yield 'The Regulars'; a brand with tone=premium and keywords=['artisan','seasonal'] might yield 'The Reserve'
downstream_usage.currency_name: draw from identity_keywords — the currency name should feel like something this brand would actually say
downstream_usage.brand_voice_summary: use tone + customer_relationship_model as the primary inputs — these two fields together define how the program should 'speak'
downstream_usage.referral_description: use tone to calibrate formality and warmth; use customer_relationship_model to frame the ask (community brands: 'bring a friend into the fold'; transactional brands: 'share your code, both of you save')
downstream_usage.reward_tiers: use price_positioning to calibrate what perks feel right — premium/luxury brands should avoid cash discounts as a primary perk; budget brands should lead with savings

sections.description: the three diagnostic questions that drive program type selection

fields.label: primary loyalty goal
fields.prompt: what is your main goal with a loyalty program?

fields.label: typical customer visit frequency
fields.prompt: how often does a typical customer visit or buy from you?

fields.label: customer spend consistency
fields.prompt: do your customers vary a lot in how much they spend, or is it pretty consistent?

options.label: pretty consistent — most customers spend about the same
varies a lot — some customers spend significantly more than others

sections.description: fields the LLM computes after all inputs are collected. never ask the user for these.

fields.label: recommendation confidence
fields.note: high = base rule fires AND industry confirms. medium = base rule fires but industry is neutral. low = conflicting signals or industry overrides — present top 2 options with explanation.

fields.label: recommendation rationale
fields.note: 1-2 sentence plain-language explanation referencing the business's specific industry, offering type, and stated goal.

decision_rules.description: apply rules in order — first match wins. each rule has a base condition set plus an optional industry_confirms list (raises confidence to high) and an industry_override block (changes the recommendation entirely when industry is a strong enough signal to override the base logic).

industry_groups.note: shorthand group labels used in rule conditions below

punch_card_eligibility.note: punch card is only viable when BOTH conditions are true. if either fails, remove punch card from consideration entirely.
conditions: offering_type is 'product' or 'service' (not 'both') — single core offering only
industry is in high_frequency_product or single_sku_service

coalition_eligibility.note: coalition should only be recommended when industry explicitly supports a shared ecosystem. never recommend coalition outside these industries.

rules.label: high frequency + varied spend
rules.rationale: high frequency creates habit; spend variance justifies tier differentiation to reward and retain top customers

rules.label: high frequency + consistent spend + acquire goal
rules.rationale: punch card is the simplest acquisition tool for high-frequency single-product businesses; if punch card is ineligible, flat points achieve the same habit loop with more flexibility

rules.label: high frequency + consistent spend + retain or revenue goal
rules.rationale: high frequency suits points accumulation; consistent spend means tiers add little value over a well-structured flat points program

rules.label: medium frequency + varied spend + revenue goal
rules.rationale: tiers motivate mid-frequency customers to increase spend to reach the next level, directly driving revenue

rules.label: medium frequency + consistent spend

industry_override.reason: price-sensitive industries see higher opt-in and engagement with immediate cash back than delayed points

rules.rationale: moderate frequency with uniform spend is the classic points program use case; cash back is preferred in price-sensitive verticals

rules.label: low frequency + varied spend + retain goal
rules.rationale: low frequency + high spend variance signals a small VIP cohort driving most revenue — tiers reward and retain them without wasting budget on occasional buyers

rules.label: low frequency + acquire goal

industry_override.reason: in experience and status-driven industries, aspirational tiers are a stronger acquisition hook than cash back — customers join to access the tier benefits, not a discount

rules.rationale: immediate tangible value is most effective at attracting new customers when visit frequency is low; in status-driven industries, tier access is the acquisition hook

rules.label: coalition — multi-brand context
rules.additional_signal: business explicitly shares a customer base with other brands (e.g. mall, airport, resort, financial product suite)
rules.note: only surface this rule if the industry is eligible AND the business context suggests a shared ecosystem. coalition should never be the first recommendation — treat it as an alternative to present alongside the base recommendation when eligible.
rules.rationale: coalition programs extend reach beyond the business's own customer base by tapping into a shared rewards network — most valuable when the customer naturally interacts with multiple brands in the same context
